title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
In November 2013, the Aam Aadmi Party published a manifesto for Delhi that promised to pass a Jan Lokpal bill within fifteen days of taking office. The word “corruption” and its cousins made up almost two percent of everything the document said. The party had grown straight out of a street movement against graft, and it talked like it. Power would be handed down to neighbourhood assemblies. Citizens would audit their own government. The broom on the party symbol was not subtle.
Twelve years later the party released its Delhi manifesto for 2025. It was a list of fifteen guarantees, mostly cash and free services, that fit on a card. Corruption was barely mentioned. Free bus rides, a monthly allowance for women, money for priests and granthis. The anti-corruption party had stopped talking about anti-corruption.
This is not an impression. I read every AAP manifesto I could find from 2013 to 2025, alongside 2,836 starred questions its MPs asked in Parliament, and measured what the party chose to talk about. The drift is not subtle either.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
The founding idea, in numbers
To track what a party cares about, count the words it spends. I built four small dictionaries, one for the anti-corruption and Swaraj language the party was founded on, one for welfare and freebies, one for development and services, and one for nationalist and religious appeals. Then I measured each manifesto by the share of its words that fell into each bucket. The method is crude on purpose. It survives the fact that the later documents are short, and it answers a simple question: of all the things AAP could talk about, which did it actually choose?
In 2013, the founding language won. Anti-corruption and Swaraj took up 1.97 percent of the Delhi manifesto, more than welfare and development combined. By 2015 it was 1.27 percent. By 2020, 1.10. By 2025 it had fallen to 0.26 percent, a collapse of almost ninety percent from where the party started. Over the same years welfare went the other way, from a third of a percent in 2013 to nearly six percent in 2025. The two lines cross.



Share of each AAP Delhi manifesto’s vocabulary falling in each theme, 2013 to 2025. The founding anti-corruption and Swaraj language fades as welfare and freebies take over. The crossover sits around 2020.


That crossover is the whole story in one picture. The party that came to power promising to make government honest left office promising to make it generous. Neither is disqualifying on its own. Plenty of good governments run on welfare. But it is a different party from the one that was sold to voters in 2013, and the change happened in plain sight, printed in its own manifestos.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
section: From a manifesto to a guarantee card
text:
From a manifesto to a guarantee card
There is a second drift hiding underneath the first, and it shows up before you read a single word. The manifestos got shorter.
The 2013 Delhi manifesto ran to about 4,400 words. The 2015 version, crowd-sourced through months of public consultations, ran longer at 5,360. Then the collapse: 1,780 words in 2020, and 396 in 2025. AAP went from a detailed seventy-point programme to a guarantee card you could photograph and forward.



AAP’s Delhi manifesto length over time. The party moved from detailed programmatic manifestos to a short list of personal guarantees.


This is what de-programmatisation looks like. A movement that once asked citizens to help write its platform ended up handing them a list of things one man promised to deliver. The branding made it explicit. By 2025 the promises were not the party’s manifesto, they were “Kejriwal ki guarantee.”
If you prefer a single number for how far the party travelled, here is one. Measure each Delhi manifesto’s vocabulary against the 2013 founding document and the distance grows every cycle: a little over half by 2015, then 0.92, then 0.97 by 2025. The 2025 manifesto has almost nothing in common, word for word, with the one that launched the party.



Cosine distance of each Delhi manifesto from the 2013 founding manifesto. Higher means less shared vocabulary. The measure is sensitive to the short later documents, so read it alongside the theme shares.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
section: What the party actually does in Parliament
text:
What the party actually does in Parliament
Manifestos are promises. Parliamentary questions are the daily work. If the drift were only campaign packaging, you might expect the party’s MPs to still raise corruption on the floor, where there are no votes to chase. They do not.
Across 2,836 starred questions asked by AAP members in the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, the most common content words are scheme, development, health, water, farmers, projects, welfare. Run the same four dictionaries over them and development and services take 2.57 percent of the vocabulary, welfare 1.33, and the founding anti-corruption language just 0.55. The party that was built to interrogate a corrupt state mostly asks it for schemes.



AAP’s parliamentary footprint. Share of its starred-question vocabulary in each theme, pooled across both houses, 2014 to 2025. Development and welfare dominate; the founding anti-corruption language is almost absent.


This is the same party showing up in a second, independent source. The manifestos and the questions are written by different people for different purposes, and they agree.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
From movement to machine
The manifestos and the questions are about words. The cleanest evidence of what AAP became is not in its language at all. It is in who it puts up for election.
Every candidate in India files a sworn affidavit declaring their assets and any criminal cases. I pulled them for every AAP candidate in Delhi, the party’s heartland, from its first contest in 2013 to its defeat in 2025.



AAP’s Delhi candidates over four elections. Median declared assets quadrupled, from about one crore rupees in 2013 to over four crore in 2025; the share carrying criminal cases rose from 7 percent to 64 percent. Source: candidate affidavits, myneta.info / ADR.


In 2013, AAP’s Delhi candidates were what the party claimed to be: modest and clean. Their median declared assets were about one crore rupees, and just seven percent had a criminal case to their name. By 2025 the median candidate was worth four and a quarter crore, four times richer, and sixty-four percent carried criminal cases. The insurgents had become, on paper, hard to tell apart from the political class they set out to replace.
One honest qualification, because AAP would rightly raise it. Part of that jump in criminal cases is the price of being an opposition party under a hostile Centre. AAP’s senior leaders were jailed in central-agency cases over this period, and many ordinary cases are the residue of protest politics. So read the criminal-case line with care. The asset line has no such defence. A party that began by asking where politicians’ money came from now fields candidates four times wealthier than its founders.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
The nationalism question, honestly
There is a popular version of the AAP story that adds a final act: a turn to soft Hindutva. Kejriwal reciting the Hanuman Chalisa, a “deshbhakti” curriculum in Delhi schools, support for building the Ram temple.
The Delhi manifestos do not support it, and for a while that left me hedging. The nationalist and religious line in the manifesto chart sits on the floor for every year, including 2025. But manifestos are written documents, in English, by committees. Kejriwal speaks, in Hindi, to crowds, and that is where the idiom would live if it lived anywhere. So I pulled the transcripts of thirty-four of his speeches from 2013 to 2026 and scored them on the same themes.



Theme share of Arvind Kejriwal’s speeches (Hindi transcripts), grouped into eras. Welfare language rises steadily; nationalist and religious language, absent from the Delhi manifestos, runs through the speeches and is highest in his years in power.


The speeches tell the manifesto story back in his own voice: welfare rising steadily across the years. And they add the part the manifestos missed. Nationalist and religious language, all but absent from the Delhi documents, runs right through the speeches, and roughly tripled from his founding years to his time in power. A recent rally speech opens, without irony, “Bharat Mata ki Jai… Vande Mataram.” The Ramrajya idiom is not a thin thread after all. It is just that he reserves it for the microphone, not the manifesto. The honest reading, with three sources now agreeing, is that AAP shed its anti-corruption identity for welfare populism, and that its leader learned to wrap that populism in the flag.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
section: What the data cannot tell you
text:
What the data cannot tell you
Two of the common claims about AAP are not text questions at all, and I will not pretend the words can settle them.
The first is that AAP was built on a grassroots cadre. The affidavit data shows what happened to its candidates, who grew richer and more entangled with the law. But the volunteers, the mohalla networks, the membership rolls, the actual cadre, do not live in any of this. The faces on the ballot changed; whether the foot soldiers did is a question for the party’s own records.
The second is that the party is finished. That is an electoral fact, not a linguistic one, and the facts trace a clean arc: a breakthrough in 2013, two landslides, then collapse.



AAP’s seats in the 70-member Delhi assembly across four elections. From 28 in its 2013 debut to back-to-back landslides of 67 and 62, then down to 22 in 2025, when it lost Delhi to the BJP. Source: Election Commission of India.


In February 2025 AAP lost Delhi, falling from 62 seats to 22, with Kejriwal and Manish Sisodia both losing their own constituencies after the party had held the city for a decade. It still governs Punjab until 2027. Whether that is the end or a trough is a question for the next election, not for a topic model.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
The public moved on too
AAP did not drift in a vacuum. The anti-corruption wave it rode crested and broke, and you can watch it happen in what India typed into Google.



Google search interest in India, 2012 to 2025. Interest in AAP’s founding demand, the Jan Lokpal, spiked around the party’s birth and then collapsed; its welfare brand, the mohalla clinic, and the wider freebie debate, revadi, rose from nothing.


Searches for “Jan Lokpal,” the bill AAP was founded to pass, surged in 2012 and 2014 and then fell to almost nothing. Over the same years, searches for “mohalla clinic,” AAP’s signature welfare scheme, and “revadi,” the slang for electoral freebies, climbed from zero. The country’s attention shifted from graft to giveaways on roughly the same timeline as AAP’s own vocabulary. The party changed with its market.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
The point
A party is allowed to change. Voters are allowed to know that it has. What the text shows is not hypocrisy so much as substitution: the anti-corruption plank that made AAP distinctive was quietly replaced by the welfare populism that makes it look like everyone else. The broom is still on the symbol. It stopped showing up in the words a while ago.

title: The Aam Aadmi Party Stopped Talking About Corruption
text:
Data and method
Manifestos. Nine AAP manifestos: Delhi 2013, 2015, 2020 and 2025; Punjab 2017 and 2022; and the Lok Sabha 2014, 2019 and 2024. Text-based PDFs were read directly; scanned documents (the 2014 Lok Sabha manifesto, the 2020 Delhi guarantee card) were recovered with OCR. Two points are news-grade rather than full documents: Delhi 2025, released only as fifteen guarantees, and Punjab 2022, which circulated as a guarantee list. The 2024 Lok Sabha vision is a short Hindi page and is used qualitatively, not in the English vocabulary counts. The two late Delhi manifestos are genuinely short, which is itself part of the finding, but it means the theme levels for those years should be read as direction, not precision.
Questions. 2,836 starred questions asked by AAP MPs, 2,567 in the Rajya Sabha and 269 in the Lok Sabha, 2014 to 2025. Rajya Sabha members were identified from a hand-checked roster of AAP’s eleven members across the Delhi and Punjab benches, because the source data’s party labels were incomplete. “Sanjay Singh” and “Harbhajan Singh” are common names and carry a small risk of contamination. The questions span a short window with changing membership, so they describe the current, post-drift party rather than a trajectory.
Measurement. Theme shares are the fraction of each document’s tokens matching four keyword dictionaries (anti-corruption and Swaraj, welfare and freebies, nationalism and religion, development and services). Cosine distance uses TF-IDF vectors over the manifesto corpus.
Candidate affidavits. Assets and criminal-case counts for every AAP candidate in Delhi 2013, 2015, 2020 and 2025 (and Punjab 2022), scraped from myneta.info, which publishes the affidavits parties’ candidates file with the Election Commission. The criminal-case figure counts candidates with one or more declared cases and does not weigh their gravity or origin.
Speeches. Thirty-four Arvind Kejriwal speech transcripts, 2013 to 2026, located via YouTube search and read through the auto-generated Hindi captions, scored on Devanagari versions of the same theme dictionaries. Speech dates are approximated by upload date, and per-year samples are small, so the speeches are grouped into eras.
Elections and public interest. Seat and vote-share figures are from the Election Commission of India. The search-interest series are from Google Trends (India, 2012 to 2025), each query smoothed over six months; Trends measures interest in the terms themselves, not in AAP specifically, so it is offered as context for the shift in salience, not as a direct measure of the party. Code and the full corpus are in the project repository.
Piyush Zaware. Built with unsupervised text analysis on AAP’s own manifestos and its MPs’ parliamentary questions.
